blocks.statement: I can use a Vernier microphone + LabQuest to time the delay between a clap and its echo from a known distance, compute v_sound = 2L/Δt, and compare to the accepted value with one credible source of error noted.

blocks.whatWeKnow: We've stated v_sound ≈ 343 m/s in air. Today we measure it ourselves.
blocks.connection: If we trust v = fλ + echo timing for everyday sound, we trust the same logic for radar pulse + Doppler analysis on Day 15.

blocks.title: Investigation 6.1 — Measure v_sound by timing the echo
blocks.markdown:
**Driving question:** Can we measure the speed of sound in our classroom by clapping toward a wall and timing the echo with a Vernier microphone?

**Equipment:** Vernier LabQuest + microphone (one per pair) · tape measure or laser distance finder · wall (or large flat surface) at least 10 m away · thermometer (room temp affects v_sound) · spreadsheet or LoggerPro for waveform analysis.

**How this lab serves the year's question:** If our v_sound is accurate to within 2% from a simple clap, NASA's Doppler radar (timing nanosecond pulses) achieves accuracy thousands of times better. Same physics, scaled.

blocks.title: What you do
steps: Choose a wall and measure L (one-way distance) to ±0.05 m. Record.
Set LabQuest sample rate to at least 10 kHz so the clap waveform is clean.
Have one partner clap sharply near the microphone. The other partner triggers data capture.
On the captured waveform, identify the clap peak + the echo peak. Read off Δt between them.
Compute v_sound = 2L / Δt. Round-trip distance is 2L.
Repeat for 3 trials. Average. Note room temperature.
Compare to expected 343 m/s + temperature correction (about +0.6 m/s per °C above 20 °C).

blocks.title: Safety
blocks.markdown: Loud claps near the microphone can damage hearing if amplified — keep volume low and don't clap directly at someone's ear.

blocks.title: Small Δt warning
blocks.markdown: A 10 m wall + a 0.06 s round trip means Δt is small. Use a sample rate ≥ 10 kHz, or use a longer corridor (15–20 m) to spread the peaks apart.

blocks.instruction: Sketch the setup: clapper + Vernier mic + wall + tape measure. Label L. The waveform shows two peaks; mark Δt between them. Compute v_sound = 2L/Δt.
prompts: Label the one-way distance L on your diagram.
Draw the captured waveform with the clap peak and the echo peak — mark Δt between them.
Write the working equation v_sound = 2L/Δt next to the waveform.

blocks.patternPrompt: CLAP-ECHO TIMING. After your trials, compute the mean v_sound and note the room temperature.
How close did your average come to 343 m/s? What's the percent difference? Was the room warmer or cooler than 20 °C?
blocks.interpretPrompt: If you could only improve ONE of (L precision, Δt precision, room temperature control) — which would shrink your error the most? And the asteroid connection: how does this serve the year's question?
blocks.frame: We measured v_sound = ___ m/s. The accepted value at room temperature is ___ m/s. Our percent difference is ___ %. The main source of error was likely ___.

blocks.prompt: If you measured v_sound = 350 m/s and your wall was 10.0 m away, what Δt did you record? Show one line of work.
